entry.title: From Prompt to Production: Creating an MCP Server for Joule Studio Using AI Core
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi, I'm Max McPhee, a BTP Solution Advisor at SAP. There are a lot of new capabilities coming to the SAP Business Suite, but what excites me most is that we can now build components for AI agents entirely within BTP and host those components in BTP—making it the one-stop shop for enterprise agents.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through how I built a simple MCP (Model Context Protocol) server from scratch, using AI-powered development tools, and deployed it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry for integration with Joule Studio. The best part? The entire development process was done using conversation development with GenAI tools from SAP. 

With "MCP" literally in my name, I was obligated to dive into building an MCP server. Now let's get started.

 

What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard originally invented by Anthropic that enables AI systems to interact with external tools and data sources in a standardized way. Think of it as a universal language that allows AI agents to discover and use tools without custom integration code for each one.

SAP has been an early supporter of MCP, recognizing its potential for enterprise AI scenarios. The company has released several open-source MCP servers to the community, including:

  • UI5 MCP Server - For SAPUI5 development assistance
  • CAP MCP Server - For SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • Fiori MCP Server - For SAP Fiori development

For more details on SAP's MCP, check out SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers to Enable Agentic Development.

With Joule Studio, you can connect your own custom MCP servers to extend Joule's capabilities with tools specific to your business needs.

 

Why would you want to do this?

Creating your own MCP server lets you extend Joule Studio with custom capabilities tailored to your business processes. You can connect to SAP systems, internal APIs, or any data source to give Joule new skills that understand your specific business context.

Requirements

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry account
  • Node.js installed locally
  • Basic understanding of JavaScript/Node.js
  • Access to Joule Studio with a SAP Build developer license

 

The Development Journey: AI All the Way Down

Here's where it gets meta. I used AI tools to build a tool for AI. Let me break down the process:

Step 1: Planning with Joule for Consultants

I started by asking Joule for Consultants to help me create a development plan for building an MCP server that could be deployed to SAP BTP. Joule helped me understand the architecture requirements, the SDK I'd need, and the configuration for Joule Studio integration.

 

Step 2: Building with an AI-Powered IDE

I took the plan from Joule and used it as the starting prompt in Cline, an AI coding assistant powered by models from SAP AI Core. The AI helped me:

  • Set up the Node.js project with the correct dependencies
  • Navigate the MCP SDK's API
  • Configure the streamable HTTP transport that Joule Studio requires
  • Discover the schemas necessary for proper argument handling

Step 3: Iterative Development

The AI didn't get everything right on the first try and that's okay! This is exactly why we are starting with a proof of concept scope. Through iterative debugging, we discovered important details like:

  • The MCP SDK requires a different schema than the plan expected
  • Session management is handled automatically via the mcp-session-id header
  • The transport must be "streamable HTTP" for Joule Studio compatibility

This is the beauty of AI-assisted development: rapid iteration with an intelligent partner. If I was doing this manually I would do the same thing, I would test what I built and address the errors I get in the logs, but with a coding assistant that work is done for me and I can focus on the outcome.

 

The Architecture

The end-to-end flow looks like this:

Joule for Consultants → AI-Powered IDE → MCP Server → BTP Cloud Foundry → Joule Studio

Key Components:

  • Runtime: BTP Cloud Foundry
  • MCP SDK: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
  • Transport: Streamable HTTP (required for Joule Studio)
  • BTP Services: Cloud Foundry runtime, Destinations service

 

Building the MCP Server

Let me share the key pieces of the implementation.

Note: The code snippets below are excerpts to illustrate the key patterns. This blog is intended to share my experience building an MCP server on BTP, not serve as a step-by-step implementation guide.

Project Dependencies

{
  "name": "mcp-echo-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node server.js"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "^1.26.0",
    "express": "^5.2.1"
  }
}

 

The Echo Tool

Here's the core pattern for registering a tool with the MCP SDK:

import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { z } from "zod";

// Define the input schema using Zod
const echoInputSchema = z.object({
  message: z.string().describe("Input message to process")
});

// Register the tool
server.tool(
  "echo",
  "Returns 'bar' when input is 'foo'. Use this to test MCP connectivity.",
  echoInputSchema.shape,
  async (args, extra) => {
    const message = args.message;

    if (message === "foo") {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: "bar" }]
      };
    }

    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Echo: ${message}` }]
    };
  }
);

Key Learning: The MCP SDK requires Zod schemas for proper argument handling. Plain JSON Schema objects won't work—a detail we discovered through debugging!

 

Cloud Foundry Manifest

---
applications:
  - name: mcp-echo-server
    memory: 512M
    disk_quota: 1G
    instances: 1
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    random-route: true
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
    health-check-type: http
    health-check-http-endpoint: /health

I've set memory to 512MB which is larger than needed for this simple echo server, but I'm using this as a MVP that I plan to expand. Starting with a reasonable production baseline avoids having to reconfigure everything later.

 

Deploying to SAP BTP

Deploying via BTP Cockpit

While you can use CF CLI I chose to do the deployment manually with the BTP Cockpit:

  1. Navigate to Cloud Foundry → Spaces → Your Space
  2. Click Deploy Application
  3. Upload your ZIP file
  4. Select manifest.yml for configuration
  5. Deploy and wait for the application to start

 

Configuring the Destination

This is critical for Joule Studio integration. Create an HTTP destination with these settings:

Property Value

Namemcp-echo-server
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://mcp-echo-server-<random>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
AuthenticationNoAuthentication
Additional Propertysap-joule-studio-mcp-server = true

Important: The URL must NOT end with /mcp—Joule Studio adds that automatically! And be sure to set the additional properties so it is visible from Joule Studio.

 

Integrating with Joule Studio

With the server deployed and destination configured:

  1. Open Joule Studio
  2. Navigate to MCP Servers
  3. Click Add MCP Server
  4. Select your destination (mcp-echo-server)
  5. Configure:
    • Name: Echo Server
    • Path: /mcp (default)
  6. Save

Now test it! Ask Joule to use your echo tool by sending "foo"—you should get "bar" back.

 

One of the features that is really neat is the ability to see the execution log so you can see the steps that the agent is taking. This is a great way to help debug if you need to provide more context in your instructions. It is very important to provide the agent with the context it needs to truly be an agent and make decisions based on the tools or Joule skills it has been provided.

 

Key Learnings & Tips

Here are the most important things I learned during this project:

  1. Zod is required: The MCP SDK v1.26+ requires Zod schemas for tool input validation. Plain JSON Schema objects will fail at runtime.

  2. Transport matters: Joule Studio requires streamable HTTP transport. SSE-type destinations are not supported.

  3. URL formatting: Your destination URL must NOT end with /mcp. Joule Studio appends this automatically.

  4. Session management: The SDK handles sessions automatically via the mcp-session-id header—you don't need to implement this yourself.

  5. Structured logging: Use JSON-formatted logs for easier debugging in BTP. The Application Logging service will thank you.

 

What's Next?

This echo server is just the beginning. You can extend this pattern to:

  • Query SAP S/4HANA data and expose it as a Joule skill
  • Connect to HANA databases for real-time analytics
  • Trigger SAP Build Process Automation workflows from natural language
  • Integrate with SuccessFactors for HR-related agent capabilities

 

Troubleshooting

If you encounter issues:

  • Server won't start: Check the Cloud Foundry logs in BTP Cockpit
  • Joule can't connect: Verify the destination URL doesn't end with /mcp
  • Tool not showing in Joule: Confirm the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server property is set to true
  • Schema validation errors: Ensure you're using Zod schemas, not plain JSON Schema

 

Conclusion

Using AI (from SAP AI Core) to build a tool for AI (Joule Studio), deployed on AI-ready infrastructure (BTP Cloud Foundry)—it's BTP all the way down! The barrier to entry for building MCP servers is lower than ever, especially with AI-assisted development tools at your fingertips.

 

Have you built an MCP server for Joule Studio? Please comment below if you have any questions or want to share your experience.

 

Have a great day and happy agent building!

-Max McPhee

Resources

  • SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers
  • Model Context Protocol Specification
  • Joule Studio Documentation
  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Documentation

entry.title: Joule Studio: Documentation for Document Grounding and Testing, Deploying & Launch Joule Agent
entry.content:
Note: Enablement of Joule Studio (IMPORTANT): Joule Studio Enablement Guide

📑 Table of Contents:

  1. Introduction
  2. What Is Document Grounding in Joule Studio?
  3. Architecture Overview
  4. Prerequisites
      SAP Services Requirements
      Access Requirements
      Required Credentials from Service Keys
  5. Setting Up Destinations
      AI Core Destination
      Object Store Destination
  6. Configuring Document Grounding in Control Tower
      Create Resource Group
  7. Monitoring Status
  8. AWS S3 CLI Guide for Uploading Documents for Grounding
  9. Viewing Grounding Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad
  10. Adding Document Grounding to Joule Studio Agents
  11. Using Joule Agent - Test in Private Environment and Deploy & Launch Joule Agent
  12. Troubleshooting

1. Introduction

Document Grounding is a capability in Joule Studio that lets you attach custom documents (PDFs, manuals, SOPs, policies, knowledge bases) to an AI agent.
This ensures the agent gives highly accurate, contextually relevant, and source-backed answers.

In older setups, admins had to manually manage:

  • Generic Secrets
  • Base64 encoding
  • Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad

Now, Joule Studio automates these tasks through Control Tower, simplifying the job for developers.

 

2. What Is Document Grounding in Joule Studio?

Document Grounding performs these functions:

  1. Reads documents stored in an S3-compatible Object Store
  2. Creates a grounding pipeline inside SAP AI Core
  3. Creates a Resource Group to store the pipeline + embeddings
  4. Makes the resource available to Joule Studio Agents

This allows the agent to:

  • Retrieve answers strictly from the grounded documents
  • Reduce hallucinations
  • Use enterprise-verified content

3. Architecture Overview

Key interactions:

  • Control Tower creates Resource Groups
  • AI Core runs embedding pipelines
  • Object Store provides documents
  • Joule Studio binds the grounding data to agents

 

4. Prerequisites

You must have:

SAP Services

  • SAP AI Core (INSTANCE - Extended Plan)
  • SAP AI Launchpad (APPLICATION - Standard Plan)
  • Object Store (INSTANCE - s3-standard or standard)
  • SAP Build Control Tower Access 
  • SAP Build Lobby with Joule Studio enabled (Joule Studio Enablement Guide)
  • Add all the roles to the user from BTP Cockpit → Security → User → (select the user(s)) → Role Collections → Assign Role Collections → Select all the roles related to that (SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, Object Store, and SAP Build )

NOTE: For Object Store plan Standard, make 2 keys with Role "Read" and "Write".

 



Access Requirements 

  • Admin role for Control Tower
  • Developer role for Joule Studio
  • Object Store admin or contributor
  • AI Core admin or contributor

Required Credentials from Service Keys

  • AI Core Service Key
  • Object Store Service Key

5. Setting Up Destinations

Two destinations must be configured:
1. AI Core Destination
2. Object Store Destination
These allow Joule Studio to perform grounding operations behind the scenes.

AI Core Destination

Step 1 — Retrieve AI Core Service Key

From BTP → Instance → Service Key

Important fields:

  • url
  • clientid
  • clientsecret
  • tokenurl (must modify)

Step 2 — Configure Destination

Destination Type: HTTP

Field

Value

URL

<AI Core Service Key URL>

Client ID

clientid

Client Secret

clientsecret

Token Service URL

<AI Core URL>/oauth/token (must append /oauth/token)

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Step 3 — Add Additional Properties

These ensure visibility in Joule Studio.

Property

Value

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-ai-core

 true

Important Note

If you forget to append /oauth/token, the grounding pipeline will not trigger.

 

Object Store Destination

Step 1 — Retrieve Object Store Service Key


Note: For Standard Plan, Use the Read role key for the destination.

Extract values such as:

  • access_key_id
  • secret_access_key
  • host
  • bucket
  • region

Step 2 — Configure Destination

Destination Field

Service Key Field

URL

host (add https://)

Client ID

access_key_id

Client Secret

secret_access_key

Token Service URL

host (add https://)

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Proxy Type

Internet

Step 3 — Additional Properties

Property

Value

region

region from service key

bucket

bucket value

username

username from service key

type

S3

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-object-store

true

These properties ensure that Joule Studio can detect the Object Store.

 

 

6. Configuring Document Grounding in Control Tower

Control Tower now includes a Joule module with Document Grounding options.

Admins decide:

  • Which Object Store to use
  • Which AI Core instance will create pipelines

6.1 Create Resource Group

Steps:

  1. Go to SAP Build Control Tower
  2. Navigate:
    Control Tower → Joule → Document Grounding
  3. Click Add
  4. Provide a Resource Group Name
  5. Select the AI Core Destination
  6. Select the Object Store Destination
  7. Click Create

A new resource group is created and grounding begins automatically.

 

7. Monitoring Status

Resource group states explained:

Status

Meaning

Pending

Pipeline running; embeddings not finished

Completed

Successfully grounded

Error

Incorrect destination or corrupted document

Troubleshooting:

  • Check destination credentials
  • Ensure documents are not corrupted
  • Ensure Object Store bucket name matches

Note:

After approximately few minute (depending on the size of the documents), the status will change to Completed.

In earlier instances, error messages occasionally appeared, or the status remained Pending for an extended period. At times, the status alternated between these messages. In such cases, it is important to ensure that the destinations are configured correctly and that the documents in the Object Store are not corrupted.

 

8. AWS S3 CLI Guide for Uploading Documents for Grounding

1. Install AWS CLI

Run the following command:

brew install awscli

2. Configure AWS CLI

After installation, open Command Prompt (Windows) or Terminal (Mac/Linux) and configure the AWS CLI using your AWS credentials.


Note: For Standard Plan, Use the Write role key for the uploading the documents.

Run the following command:

aws configure

You will be prompted to enter:

  • AWS Access Key ID
  • AWS Secret Access Key
  • Default region name (for example: eu-central-1)
  • Default output format (for example: json, or press Enter to leave it as default)

3. Upload a Grounding Document to AWS S3

To upload a local file to an S3 bucket, use the following command:

aws s3 cp <local-file-path> s3://<bucket-name>/

Example:

aws s3 cp user/…/LA.pdf s3://my-grounding-bucket/

4. Verify File Upload

To confirm that the file was uploaded successfully, list the contents of the S3 bucket:

Note: For Standard plan user again need to configure AWS CLI with read key or you can use Cyberduck for checking the uploaded docs and AWS CLI for uploading.

aws s3 ls s3://<bucket-name>/

If the file appears in the list, the upload was successful.

9. Viewing Grounding Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad

Once grounding is triggered:

  1. Go to SAP AI Launchpad
  2. Add API Connection
  3. Once loaded, go to: Workspaces → Select the resource Group

    Note: Wait for few minutes until, the resource group is created.


  4. Navigate:
    AI Core Administration → Resource Groups
  5. You will see a new group named:
    {ResourceGroupName}{UnixTimestamp}

  6. Select/Click the resource group, the go to
    Generative AI Hub → Grounding Management → Click on pipeline created.
    This confirms grounding is complete.

    Note: If the pipeline is showing "This data repository is currently empty", then check the destination and s3 bucket.

 

10. Adding Document Grounding to Joule Studio Agents

Developers now consume the resource group.

Steps:

  1. Project Creation: Open Joule Studio → Lobby → Create button → Joule Agent and Skill / Joule Skill
    Give Project Name and Description. Click on the project

  2. Create Joule Agent: Create → Joule Agent

  3. Fill all the necessary details carefully and meaningfully. 
  4. Navigate to Tools Section → Add Tools → Documents


  5. Provide:
    • Tool Name
    • Description
  6. Select one of the available Admin-created Resource Groups

The agent now has grounding enabled.

 

11. Using Joule Studio - Test in Private Environment and Deploy & Launch Joule Agent

  1. Save the Agent.
  2. Make sure Private Environment for testing must be activated: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (Environments) → Click on Private Environment Active.
  3. Enable Generative AI: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (Generative AI) → Enable Generative AI.
  4. Enable Generative AI: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (AI Agent Builder Activation) → Enable Agent Builder.
  5. Joule Agent Window → Test → Select Private → Continue.


  6. Test is woking


  7. Release the Joule Agent


  8. Make a Environment for deploying the Agent: Control Tower → Environments → Create 


  9. Deploy to created environment.


     

  10. Launch: Control Tower → Environments → Select the created environment agent_test_env → More (Joule) → Launch 


    You can use the link as per your preference.


12. Troubleshooting

Common issues:
- Wrong destination credentials
- Missing '/oauth/token'
- Corrupted PDF files in Object Store
- Resource group stuck in Pending

entry.title: SAP BPA Visibility Scenarios Explained: Monitoring and Optimize Business Processes
entry.content:
Introduction: Submitted a workflow in SAP Build Process Automation and then wondered, "Where did it go? Is it stuck? Did someone approve it yet?"
Yeah, we've all been there! That's exactly where Visibility Scenarios come to the rescue. Think of them as your center for monitoring all your business processes in real-time.

What you'll learn today:
  • How to create visibility scenarios that actually matter
  • Monitor your workflows 
  • Track down specific instances 
  • Get insights that help you optimize your processes

I have explained the visibility scenario and how to implement it in BPA in this blog.

Note: To create a Visibility Scenario, your application must have at least one process

Using a visibility scenario, you can monitor and analyze process data through the process visibility dashboard.

Step 1: Go to your project and select 'Create'; here, you will have an option for a visibility scenario

Step 2: Name your visibility scenario 'My Visibility Scenario'

Step 3: Click add button 

Step 4: Click 'Add Process'. (More options are available here; you can select whatever is needed based on your project requirements)

Step 5: Here, you can choose the process flow that needs to be added to the visibility scenario

Step 6: Click 'Save' to save the Visibility Scenario. After saving, you need to release and deploy the project. Only then will it be visible in the Control Tower.

Step 7: Go to the Control Tower > Environment, select the environment, and click the dropdown to choose the project where we created the visibility scenario.

Step 8: In that tab, at the top right corner, you have the 'Navigate to Dashboard' button you can click that.

Step 9: Once you click the 'Navigate to Dashboard' button, you will see the following output for your visibility scenario.

Note: The Instance ID in SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) Workflow and the Instance ID shown in Process Visibility are completely independent.
This means you cannot directly search for a process in Visibility using the BPA Instance ID.

However, there is a solution.
You can expose the BPA Instance ID as a custom attribute in Process Visibility. This allows you to search and filter Visibility records using the BPA Instance ID through an F4 help (value help).

Below are the steps to achieve this:

1. Create a custom variable in your BPA workflow to store the BPA Instance ID.

2.In the workflow, assign the BPA Instance ID to the newly created custom variable.

 

3.In the Visibility Scenario, create a new attribute and map it to the custom variable.
This attribute will be displayed in Process Visibility and can be used for filtering.

4. Save all changes and deploy your project so that the visibility configuration is updated.

5. After deployment, reload the Process Visibility application.
You will now see the BPA Instance ID (exposed as your custom attribute).
You can use it to search for any specific workflow execution started by the user.

Conclusion: The SAP Visibility Scenario in BPA empowers users to gain insights into business processes. With this, you can monitor open instances, failed instances, completed instances, suspended instances, and canceled instances. For open instances, you can also view their status—whether they are critical, at risk, or on track.

entry.title: SAP Build Code – Technical Series – SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery
entry.content:
Guide to CI/CD in SAP Build Code

Introduction

Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) is a crucial practice in modern software development, enabling teams to deliver high-quality code faster and more reliably. SAP Build Code provides a robust CI/CD service that integrates seamlessly with your development workflow, automating the build, test, and deployment processes.

In this guide, we'll walk through setting up and configuring a complete CI/CD pipeline for a SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application. We'll cover everything from basic setup to advanced configurations, with real examples and screenshots at every step.

What You'll Learn

  • Setting up your first CI/CD pipeline in SAP Build Code - CI/CD Service
  • Advanced job configuration with repository-based config
  • Automating builds with webhooks and triggers
  • Implementing multi-branch strategies for feature development
  • Integrating SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub Pull Requests

Prerequisites

Before we begin, ensure you have:

  • SAP BTP account with access to SAP Continuous Integration & Delivery service
  • Git repository (In this blog GitHub is going to be used)
  • Basic knowledge of CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model)
  • Node.js and npm installed locally
  • @sap/cds-dk installed

Our Sample Application

For this guide, we'll use a simple CAP application with a "Hello World" service endpoint. While minimal, this application demonstrates all the key CI/CD concepts without unnecessary complexity.

Repository structure:

cicd-service/
├── .sap_cid/
│   └── config.yaml              # CI/CD pipeline configuration
├── srv/
│   ├── hello-service.cds       # Service definition
│   └── hello-service.js        # Service implementation
├── tests/
│   └── hello-service.test.js   # Unit tests
├── jest.config.js              # Test configuration
├── mta.yaml                    # Deployment descriptor
├── package.json                # Dependencies
└── xs-security.json            # Security config

---

Part 1: Setting Up Your First CI/CD Pipeline

Step 1.1: Create Your CAP Application

First, let's look at our simple CAP application structure.

Service Definition (srv/hello-service.cds):

service HelloService {
   function sayHello() returns String;
}

Service Implementation (srv/hello-service.js):

module.exports = (srv) => {
   srv.on('sayHello', () => { return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD!'; 
   });
};

This minimal service provides a single function that returns a greeting message.

Step 1.2: Add Unit Tests

Quality assurance starts with testing. We'll use Jest to test our service.

Test File (tests/hello-service.test.js):

const cds = require('@sap/cds/lib');
const path = require('path');

describe('Hello Service', () => {
    let srv;

    beforeAll(async () => {
        // Load the CDS model
        const csn = await cds.load(path.join(__dirname, '../srv/hello-service.cds'));

        // Deploy the model to an in-memory database
        await cds.deploy(csn);

        // Get the service
        srv = await cds.serve('HelloService').from(csn);
    });

    test('sayHello returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayHello');
        expect(result).toBe('Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD!');
    });
});

Test locally:

npm install
npm test

You should see:

PASS  tests/hello-service.test.js
  Hello Service
    ✓ sayHello returns correct message (4 ms)

------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
File              | % Stmts | % Branch | % Funcs | % Lines | Uncovered Line #s
------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------
All files         |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |
 hello-service.js |     100 |      100 |     100 |     100 |
------------------|---------|----------|---------|---------|-------------------

Step 1.3: Initialize Git Repository

# Initialize Git
git init

# Add all files
git add .

# Create initial commit
git commit -m "Initial commit: Simple CAP application for CI/CD demonstration"

Step 1.4: Push to GitHub

Create a new repository on GitHub (without initializing it), then:

# Add remote (replace YOUR-USERNAME with your GitHub username)
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO.git

# Push to GitHub
git push -u origin main

Screenshot 1.1: GitHub repository overview showing all files (.sap_cid will appear later on the exercise).

Step 1.5: Access SAP Continuous Integration & Delivery

  1. Open your SAP BTP Cockpit
  2. Navigate to your subaccount
  3. Go to Services → Instances and Subscriptions
  4. Find Continuous Integration & Delivery
  5. Click to open the service

Screenshot 1.2: SAP CI/CD service in BTP Cockpit

Step 1.6: Add Your Repository

In the SAP CI/CD service:

  1. Click Repositories in the top menu
  2. Click + (Add) button
  3. Configure your repository:
  • Name: cicd-service
  • Clone URL: Your GitHub repository URL
  • Credentials: Add your GitHub credentials (I have used a Personal Access Token in this case)

Screenshot 1.3: Adding repository to SAP CI/CD

Step 1.7: Create Your First Job

Now let's create a CI/CD job using the Job Editor (GUI-based configuration):

  1. Click Jobs in the top bar.
  2. Click + (Create Job)
  3. Configure the job:
  • Job Name: cicd-service-main
  • Repository: Select cicd-service
  • Branch: main
  • Pipeline: Select Cloud Foundry Environment

Screenshot 1.5: Creating a new job in Job Editor

Step 1.8: Configure Build Stage

In the Stages tab, configure the Build stage:

  • Build Tool: mta
  • Build Tool Version: Java 21 Node 22
  • Build Descriptor: mta.yaml

Screenshot 1.6: Build stage configuration

Step 1.9: Configure Additional Unit Tests Stage

Enable and configure the Additional Unit Tests stage:

  • npm Script: test
  • Build Descriptor: package.json

Screenshot 1.7: Test stage configuration

Step 1.10: Save and Run Your First Build

  1. Click Create to save the job
  2. Click Run to trigger your first build
  3. Watch the pipeline execute!

Screenshot 1.9: Build successful with all stages passed

Click on any stage to view detailed logs.

 Congratulations! You've successfully set up your first CI/CD pipeline in SAP Build Code!

---

Part 2: Advanced Job Configuration with Source Repository Config

So far, we've used the Job Editor (GUI) to configure our pipeline. While user-friendly, it has limitations for complex configurations. Let's explore Source Repository Configuration mode, which uses a configuration file in your repository for more flexibility and version control.

2.1: Why Use Source Repository Configuration?

Benefits:

  • Configuration as code - version controlled with your application
  • More advanced options and customization
  • Easier to replicate across projects
  • Supports custom commands and scripts
  • Better for team collaboration
  • Changes tracked in Git history

2.2: Export Your Job Configuration to YAML

SAP CI/CD makes it easy to transition from Job Editor to repository configuration by allowing you to export your current settings. This is a great practice as it gives you a template to start with...

Steps to export configuration:

  1. In SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery, navigate to your job `cicd-service-main`
  2. Go to the Stages tab
  3. Click YML (next to the Job Editor button)
  4. Click Copy to copy the YAML configuration to your clipboard

Screenshot 2.1: Exporting job configuration to YAML

Screenshot 2.2: YAML configuration dialog

2.3: Understanding the Configuration Structure

Build Stage:

stages:
  build:
    buildTool: "mta"                    # Build tool (mta, npm, maven, etc.)
    buildDescriptor: "mta.yaml"         # MTA descriptor file
    buildToolVersion: "MBTJ21N22"       # MBT with Java 21 and Node 22

Malware Scan Stage:

  malwareScan:
    scan: false                 # Enable/disable security scanning

Additional Tests Stage:

  additionalTests:
    npmTests:
      buildDescriptor: "package.json"
      npmScript: "test"        # npm script to run

2.4: Create Configuration File in Repository

Now let's add this configuration to our repository. SAP CI/CD looks for configuration in `.sap_cid/config.yaml`:

Create the configuration directory and file:

# Create directory
mkdir .sap_cid

# Create config file
touch .sap_cid/config.yaml

Add the prior exported configuration to `.sap_cid/config.yaml`.

Screenshot 2.4: config.yaml file in repository

2.5: Switch Job to Source Repository Configuration

Now let's switch our job to use this repository configuration:

  1. In SAP CI/CD, navigate to your job `cicd-service-main`
  2. Click Edit
  3. In the Stages tab, find the Configuration Mode dropdown
  4. Change from Job Editor to Source Repository
  5. Click Save

Screenshot 2.5: Switching to Source Repository configuration mode

Important: Once you switch to Source Repository mode, the Job Editor will not have the configuration steps. All configuration changes must be made in the `.sap_cid/config.yaml` file and committed to Git.

2.6: Commit and Push Configuration

Let's commit our new configuration to Git:

git add .sap_cid/config.yaml
git commit -m "Add Source Repository configuration for CI/CD pipeline"
git push

2.7: Verify Configuration is Applied

Trigger a new build to verify the configuration is being read from the repository:

Screenshot 2.8: Build using Source Repository configuration

2.8: Add Custom Commands to Build Stage

Now let's customize our pipeline by adding a custom command that runs during the build stage. We'll create an npm script and execute it using the `runFirst` command. This demonstrates how to add custom logic while using the MTA build tool.

First, add a custom script to `package.json`:

{
  "name": "cicd-service",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "test": "jest",
    "build": "cds build --production",
    "build-info": "echo '=====================================' && echo 'Build Information' && echo '=====================================' && echo \"Build Date: $(date)\" && echo \"🔧 Node Version: $(node --version)\" && echo \"NPM Version: $(npm --version)\" && echo '=====================================' && echo ''"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@sap/cds": "^7",
    "express": "^4"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "jest": "^29",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^7"
  }
}

Then, update `.sap_cid/config.yaml` to call this script:

---
stages:
  build:
    buildTool: "mta"
    buildDescriptor: "mta.yaml"
    buildToolVersion: "MBTJ21N22"
    runFirst:
      command: "npm run build-info"
  malwareScan:
    scan: false
  additionalTests:
    npmTests:
      buildDescriptor: "package.json"
      npmScript: "test"

How it works:

  • `runFirst` executes commands at the beginning of the build stage (before MTA build)
  • We call `npm run build-info` which executes our custom script from package.json
  • The script displays useful build information (date, Node version, npm version)
  • Use `&&` to chain multiple commands - each subsequent command only executes if the preceding one succeeds

2.9: Commit Changes and Test

git add .sap_cid/config.yaml package.json
git commit -m "Add custom build-info script to pipeline"
git push

Run the job in CI/CD service.

Screenshot 2.12: Build information output in logs.

2.10: Advanced Configuration Options

Here are some additional configuration options you can explore, I would recommend you to check the following guides for this:

  • Configure a Cloud Foundry Environment Job in Your Repository (Stages Configuration Repository section)
  • Adding Additional Commands to Stages

2.11: Troubleshooting Configuration Issues

Build fails after switching to Source Repository:

  • Verify `.sap_cid/config.yaml` exists in repository root
  • Check YAML syntax (use a YAML validator)
  • Ensure all referenced npm scripts exist in package.json

Configuration changes not applied:

  • Make sure changes are committed and pushed to Git
  • Verify the job is in "Source Repository" mode
  • Check the branch name matches the job configuration

"Configuration file not found" error:

  • File must be named exactly `.sap_cid/config.yaml`
  • File must be in the repository root
  • Case-sensitive on Linux systems

---

Part 3: Webhook Automation & Event-Driven CI/CD

Manual builds are great for learning, but the real power of CI/CD comes from automation. Let's set up webhooks to trigger builds automatically when you push code.

3.1: Understanding Webhooks

Webhooks are HTTP callbacks that Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) send to SAP CI/CD service when events occur in your repository.

Common webhook events:

  • Push to branch
  • Pull request created/updated
  • Tag created
  • Branch created/deleted

3.2: Create Webhook Credential and Get Webhook Data

Before we can set up the webhook, we need to create a webhook credential in SAP CI/CD and get the webhook information.

Step 1: Navigate to Repository Settings

  1. In SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery, go to the Repositories tab
  2. Click on your repository (`cicd-service`)
  3. The detail view opens with a Webhook Event Receiver section

Step 2: Create Webhook Credential

In the Webhook Event Receiver section:

  1. Type: Select GitHub from the dropdown
  2. Webhook Credential: Open the matchcode, and then click on the + (plus) icon to create a new credential
  3. A dialog opens for creating the credential:
  • Name: Enter a name (e.g., `github-webhook-secret`)
  • Description: Optional description (e.g., "Webhook secret for GitHub integration")
  • Secret: Click Generate to automatically generate a secure secret. IMPORTANT: Copy the generated secret immediately! You'll need it for GitHub and won't be able to see it again
  1. Click Create to save the credential, and click on save the repository we were modifying.

Step 3: Get Webhook Data

After creating the credential, the Webhook Event Receiver section now shows:

  • Payload URL - The webhook URL for GitHub
  • Content Type - `application/json`
  • Webhook Credential - The credential you just created. As said before, now you cannot see it anymore.

Screenshot 3.5: Complete webhook data

Keep this information ready - you'll need it for the next step

3.3: Configure Webhook in GitHub

Now let's configure the webhook in your GitHub repository using the information from SAP CI/CD:

  1. Go to your GitHub repository
  2. Click on the Settings tab (repository settings, not account settings)
  3. From the left navigation pane, choose Webhooks
  4. Click Add webhook

Configure the webhook with the information from SAP CI/CD:

  • Payload URL: Paste the Payload URL from the Webhook Event Receiver section
  • Content type: Select `application/json`
  • Secret: Paste the secret you copied when creating the Webhook Credential
  • Which events would you like to trigger this webhook?
  • Select Just the push event
  • Active: Ensure this checkbox is selected

Screenshot 3.7: Webhook configuration form with SAP CI/CD data

Click Add webhook.

3.4: Test Webhook with a Code Change

Let's make a small change to trigger the webhook:

Update `srv/hello-service.js`:

module.exports = (srv) => {
    srv.on('sayHello', () => {
        return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀';
    });
};

Also change the test!

Commit and push:

git add srv/hello-service.js
git commit -m "Add rocket emoji to greeting message"
git push

3.5: Automatic Build Triggered

Within seconds, check your SAP CI/CD service - a new build should automatically start.

Screenshot 3.10: Build automatically triggered by webhook

3.6: Webhook Delivery History

You can verify webhook delivery in GitHub:

  1. Go to your repository's Settings → Webhooks
  2. Click on your webhook
  3. Click Recent Deliveries tab

Screenshot 3.12: Webhook delivery history in GitHub

3.7: Configure Scheduled/Timed Builds

Besides event-driven builds, you can also schedule builds to run periodically (e.g., nightly builds).

In SAP CI/CD job configuration:

  1. Edit your job
  2. Go to Triggers tab
  3. Enable Scheduled Builds
  4. Configure cron pattern

Example cron patterns:

  • 0 2 * * * - Every day at 2 AM
  • 0 0 * * 0 - Every Sunday at midnight
  • 0 */6 * * * - Every 6 hours

Screenshot 3.14: Scheduled build configuration

3.8: Branch-Specific Webhook Triggers

You can configure jobs to only trigger for specific branches:

In Job Configuration:

  • Branch: Use wildcards like `feature/*` or `release/*`
  • Multiple jobs can listen to the same repository on different branches

---

Part 4: Multi-Branch Strategies & Best Practices

Modern development workflows use feature branches for parallel development. Let's configure our CI/CD pipeline to handle multiple branches effectively.

4.1: Understanding Multi-Branch Workflows

Common branching strategies:

GitFlow:

  • `main` - Production code
  • `develop` - Integration branch
  • `feature/*` - Feature development
  • `release/*` - Release preparation
  • `hotfix/*` - Production fixes

GitHub Flow (simpler):

  • `main` - Production code
  • `feature/*` - All development branches

4.2: Create a Feature Branch

Let's create a feature branch for our application:

# Create and switch to feature branch
git checkout -b feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

# Verify current branch
git branch

4.3: Add New Feature to Application

Let's add a new goodbye endpoint to demonstrate multi-branch development:

Update `srv/hello-service.cds`:

service HelloService {
    function sayHello() returns String;
    function sayGoodbye() returns String;
}

Update `srv/hello-service.js`:

module.exports = (srv) => {
    srv.on('sayHello', () => {
        return 'Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀';
    });

    srv.on('sayGoodbye', () => {
        return 'Goodbye! Thanks for using SAP Build Code CI/CD! 👋';
    });
};

Add test for new endpoint (`tests/hello-service.test.js`):

const cds = require('@sap/cds/lib');
const path = require('path');

describe('Hello Service', () => {
    let srv;

    beforeAll(async () => {
        const csn = await cds.load(path.join(__dirname, '../srv/hello-service.cds'));
        await cds.deploy(csn);
        srv = await cds.serve('HelloService').from(csn);
    });

    test('sayHello returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayHello');
        expect(result).toBe('Hello World from SAP Build Code CI/CD! 🚀');
    });

    test('sayGoodbye returns correct message', async () => {
        const result = await srv.tx().send('sayGoodbye');
        expect(result).toBe('Goodbye! Thanks for using SAP Build Code CI/CD! 👋');
    });
});

Test locally:

npm test

Screenshot 4.2: Tests passing with new feature

4.4: Commit and Push Feature Branch

git add .
git commit -m "Add goodbye endpoint to HelloService"
git push -u origin feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

Screenshot 4.3: Pushing feature branch

4.5: Create Job for Feature Branches

Now let's create a CI/CD job that automatically builds all feature branches:

  1. In SAP CI/CD, go to Jobs
  2. Click + (Create Job)
  3. Configure:
  • Job Name: `cicd-service-features`
  • Repository: `cicd-service`
  • Branch:`feature/*` (using wildcard)
  • Pipeline: Cloud Foundry Environment
  • Configuration Mode: Repository Configuration

Screenshot 4.4: Creating multi-branch job with wildcard

4.6: Automatic Build for Feature Branch

The webhook automatically triggers a build for the feature branch in the next pushes to remote.

Screenshot 4.6: Feature branch build triggered

4.7: Branch-Specific Configurations

You can customize pipeline behavior per branch pattern. For feature branches, you might want a simpler configuration that skips deployment stages.

4.8: Build Retention Policies

Configure how long to keep build artifacts:

  1. Edit job
  2. Configure Build Retention:
  • Max. Builds: 50 builds
  • Keep Builds for: 7 days

Screenshot 4.9: Build retention configuration

This prevents storage bloat from feature branch builds.

4.9: Multiple Branches in Action

Let's create another feature branch to see multiple parallel builds:

# Create another feature
git checkout main
git checkout -b feature/add-timestamp

# Make a change
echo "// Feature: add timestamp" >> srv/hello-service.js

# Commit and push
git add .
git commit -m "Add timestamp feature placeholder"
git push -u origin feature/add-timestamp

Screenshot 4.10: Multiple feature branches building

4.11: Merge Feature to Main

Once feature is complete and CI passes:

# Switch to main
git checkout main

# Merge feature
git merge feature/add-goodbye-endpoint

# Push to main
git push

This triggers the main branch job automatically.

4.12: Multi-Branch Best Practices

Job Configuration:

  • Separate jobs for `main` vs `feature/*` branches
  • More thorough testing on main branch
  • Skip deployment stages for feature branches
  • Use retention policies to clean up old builds

Branch Strategy:

  • Use consistent naming (feature/*, bugfix/*, hotfix/*)
  • Keep feature branches short-lived
  • Require CI success before merging
  • Delete branches after merging

Configuration:

  • Share common config, override when needed
  • Use branch-specific configs for different behaviors
  • Document branch patterns in a README file

---

Part 5: GitHub Actions Integration - Pull Request Status Checks

While webhooks trigger SAP CI/CD builds automatically, wouldn't it be great if you could see the build status directly in your GitHub Pull Requests? In this section, we'll integrate SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub's PR checks, providing real-time feedback to developers.

5.1: The Integration Challenge

When you create a Pull Request on GitHub, it doesn't automatically know about your SAP CI/CD build status. GitHub shows its own Actions results, but the actual build happening in SAP CI/CD is invisible to the PR workflow. Knowing the status of the tests if they are passing or not, if the image is being built properly or not, if the malware scan detected some anomalous thing, might be interesting prior a merge to main branch with all the code.

What we want to achieve:

  • Automatic build status checks on Pull Requests
  • Block merging if SAP CI/CD build fails
  • Show build URL for easy debugging
  • Wait for build completion and report results

5.2: Enable API Access in SAP BTP

Step 1: Enable API access to SAP CI/CD service. This allows GitHub Actions to query build status programmatically.

Step 2: Create Service Key

  1. Click on your service CI/CD instance
  2. Go to Service Keys tab
  3. Click Create Service Key -> Service Key Name: `cicd-github-actions`
  4. Click Create

Step 3: Copy API Credentials

Click View on your service key to see the credentials. Keep this in hand, we are going to use them for the github integration.

5.3: Configure GitHub Secrets

Now we'll store the API credentials securely in GitHub Secrets.

⚠️ Importnat: GitHub may truncate secrets that contain special characters (or at least this happened to me while configuring this). The `client secret` from the service often contains special characters that can cause authentication failures.

Solution: Base64 encode the client secret before storing it in GitHub.

Step 1: Base64 Encode the Client Secret

# Replace YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET with the actual secret from service key
echo -n "YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET" | base64

Copy the output - this is your base64-encoded secret.

Step 2: Add Secrets to GitHub

  1. Go to your GitHub repository
  2. Click Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions
  3. Click New repository secret

Add the following secrets:

Secret 1: Client ID

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_CLIENT_ID`
  • Value: Your clientid from service key

Secret 2: Client Secret (Base64 Encoded)

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_CLIENT_SECRET_BASE64`
  • Value: The base64-encoded secret from Step 1

Secret 3: Token URL

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_TOKEN_URL`
  • Value: The OAuth URL from service key + `/oauth/token`
  • Example: `https://your-subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token`

Secret 4: API URL

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_API_URL`
  • Value: The ci/cd service endpoint from service key

Secret 5: Job Name

  • Name: `SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME`
  • Value: Your CI/CD job name (e.g., `cicd-service-main`)

5.4: Create GitHub Actions Workflow

Now let's create a GitHub Actions workflow that checks SAP CI/CD build status for Pull Requests.

Create `.github/workflows/sap-cicd-status.yml`:

name: SAP CI/CD Build Status Check

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  check-sap-cicd-build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    name: Check SAP CI/CD Build Status

    steps:
        - name: Get PR Commit SHA
          id: pr-info
          run: |
            echo "commit_sha=${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "branch=${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "🔍 PR Commit SHA: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}"
            echo "🌿 Branch: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}"

        - name: Get OAuth Token
          id: get-token
          run: |
            echo "🔐 Getting OAuth token..."

            # Decode base64 secret to handle special characters
            CLIENT_ID="${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_CLIENT_ID }}"
            CLIENT_SECRET=$(echo "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_CLIENT_SECRET_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d)

            echo "Client ID length: ${#CLIENT_ID}"
            echo "Client Secret length: ${#CLIENT_SECRET}"

            # Use Basic Authentication
            response=$(curl -s -X POST "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_TOKEN_URL }}" \
              -u "${CLIENT_ID}:${CLIENT_SECRET}" \
              -d "grant_type=client_credentials")

            token=$(echo "$response" | jq -r '.access_token')
            if [ "$token" == "null" ] || [ -z "$token" ]; then
              echo "❌ Failed to get OAuth token"
              echo "Response: $response"
              exit 1
            fi

            echo "✅ OAuth token obtained"
            echo "token=$token" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

        - name: Find Build for Commit
          id: find-build
          run: |
            echo "🔍 Looking for SAP CI/CD build for commit ${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}..."

            # Try to find the build (with retries in case it hasn't started yet)
            max_attempts=10
            attempt=0
            build_found=false

            while [ $attempt -lt $max_attempts ]; do
              attempt=$((attempt + 1))
              echo "Attempt $attempt/$max_attempts..."

              response=$(curl -s -X GET \
                "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_API_URL }}/v2/jobs/${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME }}/builds?filter=latest" \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ steps.get-token.outputs.token }}")

              # Extract commit hash from the latest build
              commit_hash=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].builtCommit.hash')
              build_status=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].status')
              build_result=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].result')
              build_number=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0].number')
              build_url=$(echo $response | jq -r '._embedded.builds[0]._links.ui.href')

              echo "Found build #$build_number for commit: $commit_hash"
              echo "Status: $build_status | Result: $build_result"

              if [ "$commit_hash" == "${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}" ]; then
                echo "✅ Found matching build!"
                build_found=true
                echo "build_status=$build_status" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_result=$build_result" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_number=$build_number" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "build_url=$build_url" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                break
              fi

              echo "⏳ Build not found yet, waiting 10 seconds..."
              sleep 10
            done

            if [ "$build_found" = false ]; then
              echo "❌ Could not find build for commit ${{ steps.pr-info.outputs.commit_sha }}"
              exit 1
            fi

        - name: Wait for Build Completion
          id: wait-build
          if: steps.find-build.outputs.build_status != 'FINISHED'
          run: |
            echo "⏳ Build is not finished yet. Waiting for completion..."

            max_wait=1800  # 30 minutes
            elapsed=0
            interval=30

            while [ $elapsed -lt $max_wait ]; do
              response=$(curl -s -X GET \
                "${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_API_URL }}/v2/jobs/${{ secrets.SAP_CICD_JOB_NAME }}/builds/${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_number }}" \
                -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ steps.get-token.outputs.token }}")

              status=$(echo $response | jq -r '.status')
              result=$(echo $response | jq -r '.result')

              echo "Status: $status | Result: $result"

              if [ "$status" == "FINISHED" ]; then
                echo "✅ Build completed!"
                echo "final_status=$status" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                echo "final_result=$result" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
                break
              fi

              echo "Still running... (${elapsed}s elapsed)"
              sleep $interval
              elapsed=$((elapsed + interval))
            done

            if [ $elapsed -ge $max_wait ]; then
              echo "⚠️  Timeout waiting for build to complete"
              exit 1
            fi

        - name: Check Build Result
          run: |
            # Use the final result if we waited, otherwise use the found result
            result="${{ steps.wait-build.outputs.final_result }}"
            if [ -z "$result" ] || [ "$result" == "null" ]; then
              result="${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_result }}"
            fi

            echo "📊 Final Build Result: $result"
            echo "🔗 Build URL: ${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_url }}"

            # Check for successful build results
            if [ "$result" == "SUCCESS" ] || [ "$result" == "FINISHED" ]; then
              echo "✅ SAP CI/CD build passed!"
              exit 0
            else
              echo "❌ SAP CI/CD build failed with result: $result"
              echo ""
              echo "Please check the build details at:"
              echo "${{ steps.find-build.outputs.build_url }}"
              exit 1
            fi

5.5: How It Works

Let's break down what this workflow does:

Step 1: Get PR Commit SHA

  • Extracts the commit hash from the Pull Request
  • This is used to match against SAP CI/CD builds

Step 2: Get OAuth Token

  • Obtains OAuth access token for API calls

Step 3: Find Build for Commit

  • Polls SAP CI/CD API for the latest build
  • Matches the commit SHA with the PR commit
  • Retries up to 10 times (builds might not start immediately)
  • Extracts build status, result, number, and URL

Step 4: Wait for Build Completion

  • If build is still running, polls every 30 seconds
  • Maximum wait time: 30 minutes
  • Updates when build status becomes "FINISHED"

Step 5: Check Build Result

  • Evaluates the final build result
  • Provides build URL for debugging failures
  • Exits with error code if build fails

5.6: Commit and Test the Workflow

# Add the workflow file
git add .github/workflows/sap-cicd-status.yml

# Commit
git commit -m "Add GitHub Actions integration for SAP CI/CD build status"

# Push to main
git push

5.7: Test with a Pull Request

Let's test the integration by creating a Pull Request:

# Create a feature branch
git checkout -b feature/test-github-integration

# Make a small change
echo "// Test GitHub integration" >> srv/hello-service.js

# Commit and push
git add srv/hello-service.js
git commit -m "Test: GitHub Actions integration"
git push -u origin feature/test-github-integration

Now create a Pull Request on GitHub targeting the `main` branch.

Screenshot 5.6: Pull Request with status check running

5.8: Watch the Magic Happen

The workflow will:

  1. Trigger automatically when PR is created
  2. Wait for SAP CI/CD build to start
  3. Poll build status until completion
  4. Report results back to the PR

Screenshot 5.7: PR status check successful

5.9: Build Failure Scenario

If the SAP CI/CD build fails (e.g., tests fail, build errors), the GitHub status check will fail and block merging.

The workflow provides the build URL so developers can investigate:

❌ SAP CI/CD build failed with result: FAILURE Please check the build details at: https://cicd-service.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/ui/index.html#/builds/123

5.10: Advanced Configurations

- Configure Branch Protection Rules

To enforce build status before merging:

  1. Go to Repository Settings → Branches
  2. Add rule for `main` branch
  3. Enable Require status checks to pass before merging
  4. Select Check SAP CI/CD Build Status

- Customize Timeout and Retry Values

Adjust polling behavior in the workflow:

# In "Find Build for Commit"
step max_attempts=10 # Number of retries to find
build sleep 10 # Seconds between retries

# In "Wait for Build Completion"
step max_wait=1800 # Maximum wait time (30 minutes)
interval=30 # Poll interval (30 seconds)

- Add Status Badges to your README file

Add a status badge showing CI/CD status in your README.md:

[![SAP CI/CD Status](https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO/workflows/SAP%20CI%2FCD%20Build%20Status%20Check/badge.svg)](https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO/actions)

5.12: Benefits of This Integration

Developer Experience:

  • See build status directly in Pull Requests
  • Block merging of broken code automatically
  • Quick access to build logs via provided URL
  • No need to switch between GitHub and SAP CI/CD UI

Team Benefits:

  • Enforce quality gates before merge
  • Reduce manual review overhead
  • Catch issues earlier in development cycle
  • Maintain code quality standards

DevOps Benefits:

  • Single source of truth for build status
  • Automated enforcement of CI/CD checks
  • Audit trail of all build results
  • Integration with GitHub's native PR workflow

---

Conclusion & Next Steps

Congratulations! You've successfully set up a comprehensive CI/CD pipeline for your SAP CAP application using SAP Build Code's Continuous Integration & Delivery service, complete with GitHub Actions integration!

What We've Accomplished

In this guide, we covered:

  • Part 1: Set up first CI/CD pipeline with a simple CAP application
  • Part 2: Mastered advanced configuration with repository-based config
  • Part 3: Automated builds with webhooks and event-driven triggers
  • Part 4: Implemented multi-branch strategies for parallel development
  • Part 5: Integrated SAP CI/CD build status with GitHub Pull Requests

Personal Thoughts

In today's competitive landscape, speed of delivery isn't just an advantage, it's a necessity. Automation is the key to
staying agile. The more you automate, the faster you can deliver enhancements and new features to your users.

I strongly encourage you to implement this CI/CD setup and evaluate which projects in your portfolio could benefit from
it. The return on investment is substantial, by automating your deployment pipeline, you eliminate significant
operational overhead and free your team to focus on what truly matters: writing correct, secure, and functional code.
Let the CI/CD service handle the heavy lifting of cloud deployments.

SAP's documentation for the Continuous Integration & Delivery service is comprehensive and includes helpful video
tutorials that guide you through the configuration process step-by-step. Getting started is easier than you might
think.

Stop deploying manually. Start shipping smarter. Your future self (and your team) will thank you.

I am thinking about a post of integrating this service with Cloud Transport Management Service in BTP. I will start considering it and working on it.

See you in the next blog!

Nata.

entry.title: Introducing prebuilt Joule skills in SAP Build digital workspaces: Get more done with simple prompts
entry.content:
If your day involves spinning up new spaces, hunting for the right workspace, or sharing content with your team, you know the clicks can add up. SAP Build simplifies this in its digital workspace experience with new prebuilt Joule skills which turns plain‑language prompts into actions. Ask Joule to create or find a workspace and it does the work in the background- then gives you a direct link in the chat so you can jump right in

The first set of prebuilt Joule skills are already live. Take a look at what’s available today: 

 

What’s in this first set of skills

  • Create workspaces from a prompt
    • Example: “Create a workspace called ‘Q3 Onboarding Hub’ using the standard onboarding template.”
    • Joule creates and names the workspace behind the scenes and returns a link in the chat.
  • Find workspaces you need
    • Example: “Show me all workspaces I’m a member of related to ‘Onboarding’.”
    • Joule lists relevant workspaces right in the chat, helping you get where you need to go faster.
  • Add documents right from chat
    • Drag and drop a file into the Joule chat, confirm the target workspace, and Joule adds it for you- no tab hopping required.
  • Find and add members to workspaces
    • Tell Joule who you want to add to a workspace, and Joule finds the person, confirms and sends them an invitation to join the workspace.

Why this matters

For teams needing a space to collaborate and produce results, these skills simplify their day-to-day so that -

  • members spend less time on common setup tasks like launching a project hub, onboarding center, or campaign space,
  • users can move from “search and navigate” to “ask and act” without leaving their flow of work,
  • workspace creators get consistency in content through standardized workspace templates to get started quickly, 
  • business users get more self-service options and admins get fewer “how do I create/find X?” tickets.

Prebuilt skills can be used in conjunction with the existing AI capabilities already available in digital workspaces. For example, once you find or create workspaces via Joule, you can use embedded AI assistance to create and publish content or summarize documents within the workspace.

Further, Joule can be enabled with document grounding for workspaces so that it answers questions based on content maintained in workspaces and links back to sources. This way its responses are grounded and relevant to business context.

Admins and developers can also build custom skills for Joule using Joule Studio and use them in digital workspaces.

Everyday scenarios where you can use these skills:

  • HR onboarding hub: “Create a workspace called ‘New Hire Hub – July’ based on the onboarding template.” Share the link with HR/ team managers in minutes.
  • Project kickoff: “Find all workspaces mentioning ‘Apollo Launch’.” Jump straight into the right workspace for plans, assets, and status.
  • Policy refresh: “Create a workspace named ‘FY26 Policy Updates’.” Drag and drop updated PDFs into the chat to populate the workspace content folder quickly.
  • Campaign workspace: “Create ‘Q2 Partner Enablement’.” Then use AI writing assistance on the landing page to publish a clean, on‑brand overview.
  • Knowledge triage: “Show my ‘Customer Escalations’ workspaces.” Get a list in chat and dive into the relevant one to update content or add the right members to solve the issue.

Getting started with Joule’s prebuilt skills and embedded AI in digital workspaces:

    • First, the admin must ensure Work Zone is enabled as a Joule launcher as per the instructions in this guide
    • Once set up is done, open Joule in SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition or SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone and try prompts like
      • “Create a workspace named ‘Team All‑Hands’.”
      • “Find my workspaces related to ‘Compliance’.”
    • Ask your admin to enable document grounding for the workspaces you rely on- so Joule can answer questions with citations.
    • Ask your admin to enable embedded AI features to use AI assistance when editing pages or posts to speed up content creation once your workspace is ready or to summarize your documents quickly. See how you can use embedded AI for document summarization or workspace content creation here: 

 

Tips for Best Results for interacting with Joule:

  • Be specific with search keywords while searching for workspaces or content with Joule
  • While prompting give sufficient context to help Joule understand the task.

What’s next
This is just the first set of prebuilt skills we’re releasing for digital workspaces. We’re continuing to expand the catalog with more actions and smarter multi-step flows.

entry.title: SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 – Brazil Edition: La Comunidad SAP Paraguay Estuvo Ahí
entry.content:
¡Hola comunidad SAP Paraguay! En marzo de 2026 tuvimos la oportunidad de participar en el SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 – Brazil Edition, dos jornadas intensas de aprendizaje hands-on en São Paulo, Brasil. Y como siempre, Paraguay no se quedó afuera.



¿Qué es el SAP CodeJam?
Para quienes aún no lo conocen: el SAP CodeJam es un evento práctico de entre 5 y 6 horas, respaldado por SAP, donde los participantes exploran tecnologías reales en un ambiente colaborativo y sin presión. No se trata de escuchar presentaciones — se trata de hacer, experimentar y aprender juntos.

Esta edición fue parte de un roadshow por Brasil, con paradas en la oficina de SAP en São Paulo y Exed Consulting. Los instructores fueron los Developer Advocates Antonio Maradiaga y Kevin Riedelsheimer — un lujo para cualquier developer SAP.


Los dos dias ¿Qué vivimos?
✔️3 de marzo – Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding
El primer día nos introdujo a Joule Studio, la nueva herramienta dentro de SAP Build que permite extender a Joule, el asistente digital de SAP, con flujos propios.

✔️ 4 de marzo – SAP Build: Create Event-Based Processes
El segundo día fue igualmente intenso. En Exed Consulting, trabajamos con SAP Build Process Automation y SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh para construir procesos disparados por eventos.

Lo mas destacado
Más allá del contenido técnico, lo que hace especial a un CodeJam es la gente. Compartir mesa con profesionales de Brasil, resolver ejercicios juntos, debatir enfoques distintos — eso no tiene precio.
Un agradecimiento especial a Kevin Riedelsheimer, Antonio Maradiaga y Antonia Zorn por la confianza y la oportunidad


SAP Community = Conexiones Reales
Eventos como este no llegan solos. Detrás de cada CodeJam hay una comunidad activa que lo hace posible: profesionales que publican en el SAP Community Blog, que comparten sus experiencias, que asisten a eventos en la región, que conectan con Developer Advocates y que demuestran que hay una comunidad con interés real.

No alcanza con querer que suceda: hay que ser parte activa. Eso significa:

  • Crear una cuenta en SAP Community y participar con preguntas, respuestas y blogs
  • Seguir y conectar con los Developer Advocates en LinkedIn y SAP Community
  • Compartir las experiencias de los eventos regionales para que Paraguay aparezca en el radar
  • Asistir a CodeJams en países vecinos y llevar de vuelta el conocimiento.

Desde la SAP Community Paraguay seguimos trabajando para que Ciudad del Este y Asunción sean paradas reales en el mapa de la comunidad SAP de la región.


¿Qué tema te gustaría ver en un CodeJam en Paraguay? ABAP Cloud, Joule Studio, SAP Build, BTP, UI5 — cuéntanos en los comentarios. Cada opinión cuenta para hacerlo realidad.

¡Sigamos construyendo comunidad! 🚀

entry.title: Old Tile Still Appearing in SAP Build Work Zone After Complete Re‑Exposure from S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am facing an issue with federated content from an on‑premise S/4HANA system to SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition).
Our authorization team has removed the old tile and assigned a new tile to the correct custom role, custom catalog, and custom group in S/4HANA.

To ensure a clean setup, I deleted the previous exposure configuration and re‑created the full setup from scratch in:

  • /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE (content exposure)
  • SAP BTP → Build Work Zone → Content Provider → Synchronize Content

Despite repeating the full process multiple times, the old tile continues to appear in Build Work Zone instead of the new one.
The authorization consultant has verified both the new role and the tile assignments several times on the S/4HANA side, and everything looks correct there — but Build Work Zone keeps federating only the old tile.

Has anyone experienced something similar?

I am trying to understand:

  • Why outdated tile information is still being federated
  • Whether additional cleanup is required on the CDM or content provider side
  • If there are any cache layers (S/4HANA, BTP, or Build Work Zone) that must be explicitly cleared
  • Whether Exposure Version 2 requires specific steps to refresh or replace previously published app IDs

Any advice, known issues, or guidance on how to force Build Work Zone to pick up the updated tile would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Pregúntame lo que quieras con SAP Champions – Serie mensual (Sesión 2)
entry.content:
Hola querida SAP Community,

Mi nombre es María Antonieta López, SAP Champion de México.

Actualmente, trabajo como Directora de Programas SAP, enfocada en programas globales de transformación SAP, implementaciones de SAP S/4HANA, arquitectura empresarial, marcos de gobernanza e innovación digital dentro del ecosistema SAP.

Como parte de los SAP Champions que lideran la iniciativa “Ask Me Anything” junto con Sebastiano Marchesini,

Me complace informarles que yo estaré liderando la edición de marzo.🚀

Así que sí…

Pueden preguntarme. LO QUE SEA.

 

Después de más de 20 años viviendo dentro de transformaciones SAP, probablemente he visto lo bueno, lo malo… y esos momentos de “¿cómo sobrevivió este sistema al go-live?” 😅

 

Durante las próximas semanas estaré respondiendo cualquier pregunta que tengan — sin filtros — solo SAP en vivo y real.

💡Podemos explorar temas como:

⚙️Implementaciones de SAP S/4HANA y programas globales de transformación – Ejecución de implementaciones end-to-end de SAP S/4HANA y programas de transformación a gran escala, permitiendo a las organizaciones modernizar sus procesos de negocio centrales, estandarizar operaciones globales y adoptar capacidades digitales de nueva generación.

🏗️ Arquitectura Empresarial – Procesos, artefactos y estrategias para la arquitectura empresarial de SAP basada en TOGAF.

🧼 Clean Core en SAP Cloud ERP (Private & Public) — procesos de gestión de proyectos para implementar o crear extensiones o interfaces en tu proyecto.

(Hablando de BUILD para configuradores y su uso con Joule, APIs preconstruidas, APIs nuevas, y por supuesto nuestra plataforma como servicio SAP Integration Suite, todo dentro de nuestra querida SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)).

☁️ RISE o GROW con SAP + Gobernanza y metodología Clean Core

Desde SAP Activate hasta marcos como SAP Application Extension Methodology, ISA-M (Integration Solution Advisory Methodology), SAP Design Thinking, SAP Data Architecture Framework y, por qué no… incluso SAP Business AI Framework.

Puedes preguntar sobre qué artefactos usar, cómo ejecutar los procesos o por qué tengo que hacer esta tarea... Y te diré lo que pasará si te los saltas... Muahaha! …💀 así que mis historias de terror, como el invierno… están por llegar.

🏛️ Cómocrear un SAP Center of Excellence (CoE) o brindar servicios de SAP AM (Application Management) y realmente vivir felices para siempre con SAP usando Cloud ALM + Focused Build + Activate.

🌍 Globalización en SAP, ya sea de forma general o por país. ¡Larga vida a SAP Document & Reporting Compliance!

Y también a las extensiones DRC de nuestros partners SAP.

🧭 Roadmaps de soluciones y estrategia de transformación, etc

¿Y cualquier otra cosa? y ... ¡si no sé la respuesta! 👉 Prometo encontrar a alguien que sí la sepa… para ustedes.

 

Ya tenemos todo listo. 💬 Ahora es tu turno.

Deja tu pregunta en la sección de comentario👇 o puedes preguntar de forma anónima aquí 👉 AMA Champion Anonymously Questions o escaneando el QR Code 

 

📅Esta edición del AMA estará abierta para preguntas hasta el 27 de marzo de 2026.

Después de esa fecha, las preguntas seleccionadas serán respondidas en un video grabado que se publicará en el canal de YouTube de la SAP Community  👉 @SAPCommunity

También se publicará un blog resumen con las preguntas y respuestas para la comunidad.

 

Cuéntame…

🤓¿Qué tema de SAP te gustaría que exploremos juntos?

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 2)
entry.content:
Hey dear SAP Community,

My name is Maria Antonieta Lopez,  I currently work as an SAP program director focusing on global SAP transformation programs, SAP S/4HANA implementations, enterprise architecture, governance frameworks, and digital innovation within the SAP ecosystem.

As an SAP Champion myself, I’ll be leading the March edition for the Ask Me Anything series, which we started together with my dear friend Sebastiano Marchesini.

So yes…

You can ask. Me. ANYTHING.

After 20+ years living inside SAP transformations, I’ve probably seen the good, the bad… and the “how did this system survive go-live?” moments. 😅

So over the next weeks I’ll be answering any question you have -- No filters - Just real SAP Live --.

We can talk about which format to use, or the process for something, or even… why I have to do this task.... and I'll tell you what will happen if you don't...

And... if I don't know the answer? 👉 I promise I'll find someone who does... for you.

 

We have everything ready...  💬 Now it’s your turn.
